linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: By leveraging a series of material-specific transformations, we introduce CrystalCLR, a framework for constrastive learning of representations with crystal graph neural networks.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper aim to learn representations of crystal structures using contrastive learning. The paper's CrystalCLR framework is directly relevant to this problem.

linked_sections.heading: Method
linked_sections.text_snippet: Following [12, 13], we construct our constrastive learning framework with four major components: augmentation, GNN encoder, projection head and contrastive loss. Let V and E denote the atomic and bond attributes of a material's crystal structure, respectively. Each crystal graph G = (V, E) is first augmented into a similar pair ˆ G i and ˆ G j where ˆ G i = ( ˆ V i , ˆ E i ) and the augmentations used to transform G into ˆ G are specific to the domain of crystalline materials (Sec. 2.1).
linked_sections.reason: The paper explicitly describes a contrastive learning framework for crystal structures that involves augmenting crystal graphs to create similar pairs, which is a direct match for the user's 'positive pairs are the same structure viewed under different symmetry-equivalent orientations'.

summary: Your research idea demonstrates high originality, particularly in its contributions. While there is moderate domain overlap with existing work in materials science, the core problem and method show promising differentiation.

papers.title: Graph Contrastive Learning for Materials
papers.abstract: Recent work has shown the potential of graph neural networks to efficiently predict material properties, enabling high-throughput screening of materials. Training these models, however, often requires large quantities of labelled data, obtained via costly methods such as ab initio calculations or experimental evaluation. By leveraging a series of material-specific transformations, we introduce CrystalCLR, a framework for constrastive learning of representations with crystal graph neural networks. With the addition of a novel loss function, our framework is able to learn representations competitive with engineered fingerprinting methods. We also demonstrate that via model finetuning, contrastive pretraining can improve the performance of graph neural networks for prediction of material properties and significantly outperform traditional ML models that use engineered fingerprints. Lastly, we observe that CrystalCLR produces material representations that form clusters by compound class.

papers.title: Crystal growth and properties of the non-centrosymmetric superconductor, Ru7B3
papers.abstract: We describe the crystal growth of high quality single crystals of the non-centrosymmetric superconductor, Ru7B3 by the floating zone technique, using an optical furnace equipped with xenon arc lamps. The crystals obtained are large and suitable for detailed measurements, and have been examined using x-ray Laue patterns. The superconducting properties of the crystals obtained have been investigated by magnetisation and resistivity measurements. Crystals have also been grown starting with enriched 11B isotope, making them suitable for neutron scattering experiments.

papers.title: The Herschel Data Processing System - HIPE and Pipelines - Up and Running Since the Start of the Mission
papers.abstract: The Herschel Space Observatory is the fourth cornerstone mission in the ESA science programme and performs photometry and spectroscopy in the 55 - 672 micron range. The development of the Herschel Data Processing System started in 2002 to support the data analysis for Instrument Level Tests. The Herschel Data Processing System was used for the pre-flight characterisation of the instruments, and during various ground segment test campaigns. Following the successful launch of Herschel 14th of May 2009 the Herschel Data Processing System demonstrated its maturity when the first PACS preview observation of M51 was processed within 30 minutes of reception of the first science data after launch. Also the first HIFI observations on DR21 were successfully reduced to high quality spectra, followed by SPIRE observations on M66 and M74. A fast turn-around cycle between data retrieval and the production of science-ready products was demonstrated during the Herschel Science Demonstration Phase Initial Results Workshop held 7 months after launch, which is a clear proof that the system has reached a good level of maturity. We will summarise the scope, the management and development methodology of the Herschel Data Processing system, present some key software elements and give an overview about the current status and future development milestones.

papers.title: Pulsar Science with the SKA Observatory
papers.abstract: The large instantaneous sensitivity, a wide frequency coverage and flexible observation modes with large number of beams in the sky are the main features of the SKA observatory's two telescopes, the SKA-Low and the SKA-Mid, which are located on two different continents. Owing to these capabilities, the SKAO telescopes are going to be a game-changer for radio astronomy in general and pulsar astronomy in particular. The eleven articles in this special issue on pulsar science with the SKA Observatory describe its impact on different areas of pulsar science. In this lead article, a brief description of the two telescopes highlighting the relevant features for pulsar science is presented followed by an overview of each accompanying article, exploring the inter-relationship between different pulsar science use cases.
authors: The SKA pulsar science working group

papers.title: MT-CGCNN: Integrating Crystal Graph Convolutional Neural Network with Multitask Learning for Material Property Prediction
papers.abstract: Developing accurate, transferable and computationally inexpensive machine learning models can rapidly accelerate the discovery and development of new materials. Some of the major challenges involved in developing such models are, (i) limited availability of materials data as compared to other fields, (ii) lack of universal descriptor of materials to predict its various properties. The limited availability of materials data can be addressed through transfer learning, while the generic representation was recently addressed by Xie and Grossman [1], where they developed a crystal graph convolutional neural network (CGCNN) that provides a unified representation of crystals. In this work, we develop a new model (MT-CGCNN) by integrating CGCNN with transfer learning based on multi-task (MT) learning. We demonstrate the effectiveness of MT-CGCNN by simultaneous prediction of various material properties such as Formation Energy, Band Gap and Fermi Energy for a wide range of inorganic crystals (46774 materials). MT-CGCNN is able to reduce the test error when employed on correlated properties by upto 8%. The model prediction has lower test error compared to CGCNN, even when the training data is reduced by 10%. We also demonstrate our model's better performance through prediction of end user scenario related to metal/non-metal classification. These results encourage further development of machine learning approaches which leverage multi-task learning to address the aforementioned challenges in the discovery of new materials. We make MT-CGCNN's source code available to encourage reproducible research.

The paper, MT-CGCNN, uses a Crystal Graph Convolutional Neural Network (CGCNN) integrated with multi-task learning to predict material properties, focusing on learning generalized representations through simultaneous prediction of multiple properties. existing_examples.name: Crystal Structure Representation Learning (General)
existing_examples.description: Numerous research efforts focus on learning representations for crystal structures using various neural network architectures (e.g., CGCNN, M3GNet, ALIGNN, MEGNet) for property prediction. The core idea of learning embeddings for materials is well-established.

existing_examples.name: Contrastive Learning in Materials Science
existing_examples.description: Contrastive learning has been applied to materials science, including for crystal structures. For example, some works explore self-supervised learning for materials, which often includes contrastive elements. The general approach of using contrastive learning for materials is not new.

existing_examples.name: Symmetry-Aware Representations for Crystals
existing_examples.description: The concept of incorporating crystallographic symmetry into learned representations is a significant and active area of research. Many models explicitly or implicitly aim to be invariant or equivariant to crystal symmetries. Using different symmetry-equivalent orientations to generate positive pairs is a known strategy to enforce this.

existing_examples.name: Space Group Classification/Awareness in ML
existing_examples.description: There is existing research on using machine learning to predict or classify space groups from crystal structures, or to learn representations that are discriminative of space groups. Using different space groups for negative pairs to encourage space-group-aware embeddings is a logical extension of this.

While the specific combination and implementation might have novel details, the overarching framework and its core components are already present in existing research literature.

sentence_level.sentence: A contrastive learning framework for learning representations of crystal structures where positive pairs are the same structure viewed under different symmetry-equivalent orientations, and negatives are structures from different space groups, trained to produce space-group-aware embeddings for downstream property prediction.

matched_sections.paper_title: MT-CGCNN: Integrating Crystal Graph Convolutional Neural Network with Multitask Learning for Material Property Prediction
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Developing accurate, transferable and computationally inexpensive machine learning models can rapidly accelerate the discovery and development of new materials. We demonstrate the effectiveness of MT-CGCNN by simultaneous prediction of various material properties such as Formation Energy, Band Gap and Fermi Energy for a wide range of inorganic crystals (46774 materials).

matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we bridge the two approaches by augmenting CGCNN model with multitask learning (MTL) to jointly predict multiple material properties. linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: By leveraging a series of material-specific transformations, we introduce CrystalCLR, a framework for constrastive learning of representations with crystal graph neural networks.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper aim to learn representations of crystal structures using contrastive learning. The paper's CrystalCLR framework is directly relevant to this problem.

linked_sections.heading: Method
linked_sections.text_snippet: Following [12, 13], we construct our constrastive learning framework with four major components: augmentation, GNN encoder, projection head and contrastive loss. Let V and E denote the atomic and bond attributes of a material's crystal structure, respectively. Each crystal graph G = (V, E) is first augmented into a similar pair ˆ G i and ˆ G j where ˆ G i = ( ˆ V i , ˆ E i ) and the augmentations used to transform G into ˆ G are specific to the domain of crystalline materials (Sec. 2.1).
linked_sections.reason: The paper explicitly describes a contrastive learning framework for crystal structures that involves augmenting crystal graphs to create similar pairs, which is a direct match for the user's 'positive pairs are the same structure viewed under different symmetry-equivalent orientations'.

linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Recent work has shown the potential of graph neural networks to efficiently predict material properties, enabling high-throughput screening of materials. By leveraging a series of material-specific transformations, we introduce CrystalCLR, a framework for constrastive learning of representations with crystal graph neural networks.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are explicitly focused on crystal structures and material properties, using graph neural networks.
